research.primary_citation: Hershfield et al. (2011) — fMRI shows the brain represents your future self identically to how it represents a stranger. Delegating problems to 'tomorrow you' is neurologically equivalent to delegating to someone else.
research.supporting_citation: Sirois & Pychyl (2016) — procrastination is an emotion regulation failure, not a time management failure. Tasks pushed 3-4× more often have aversive emotional valence: ambiguity, self-doubt, fear of failure.
research.behavior_change: Name the specific obstacle (WOOP method, Oettingen 2014). The question 'what makes this uncomfortable?' is the intervention — not adding motivation.

coach_message_template: '{task_name}' has been pushed {push_count} times. Research from UCLA found your brain represents your future self the same way it represents a stranger — which is why handing problems to 'tomorrow you' feels so easy. What usually makes this task uncomfortable when it comes up?

research.primary_citation: Steel (2007) Temporal Motivation Theory meta-analysis (691 correlations) — temporal distance is the single strongest predictor of procrastination, stronger than task difficulty, self-regulation, or self-efficacy. Formula: Motivation = (Expectancy × Value) / (Impulsivity × Delay). No deadline means Delay → ∞, Motivation → 0.
research.supporting_citation: Even a self-imposed internal milestone activates the motivational math. The deadline does not need to be external or binding.
research.behavior_change: Set any concrete milestone — even a personal one with no external accountability. The act of specifying a date changes the motivational arithmetic.

coach_message_template: '{task_name}' doesn't have a deadline. Piers Steel's procrastination meta-analysis — the largest ever done — found deadline distance is the single strongest predictor of a task never getting done, more than difficulty or how busy you are. Even an internal milestone this week activates the motivational math.

research.primary_citation: Kahneman & Tversky (1979, 1982) Planning Fallacy — people systematically underestimate task duration and ignore base rates. This bias does not improve with expertise, feedback, or effort. Domain experts show the same magnitude of error as novices.
research.supporting_citation: Buehler, Griffin & Ross (1994) — the structural fix is reference class forecasting: use your own historical completion rate as the planning anchor, not an optimistic estimate for this particular week.
research.behavior_change: Use your own past completion rate as the schedule anchor. If you've been completing 60% of what you plan, schedule 60% of what feels right.

coach_message_template: You've completed about {completion_pct}% of what you've scheduled this week. Nobel Prize research found this pattern is universal — even experts who know about the bias show it. The fix isn't willpower; it's using your own history as an anchor.

research.primary_citation: Lally et al. (2010) UCL study (96 participants, 12 weeks) — average habit automaticity takes 66 days, not 21. Range: 18–254 days. First 30 days show the highest fragility — incomplete neural consolidation means the habit is still effortful and context-dependent.
research.supporting_citation: Wood (2014) — fragility during formation correlates most strongly with context-cue consistency (same time, same place), not motivation or intention. Missing the cue does more damage than missing a day of motivation.
research.behavior_change: A 5-minute version of the habit preserves the cue-routine neural link even when full execution is not possible. Minimum viable execution beats skipping entirely.

coach_message_template: '{habit_name}' has slipped below your target cadence for two weeks. UCL research found the first 30 days are the most fragile — not because of motivation, but because the neural circuit is still consolidating. Even a 5-minute version this week keeps the cue-routine link alive.

research.primary_citation: Masicampo & Baumeister (2011) Florida State — the Zeigarnik effect (open tasks occupy working memory) is relieved by making a plan, not by completing the task. A concrete next step closes the cognitive loop as effectively as completion.
research.supporting_citation: Each unattended open loop consumes a continuous slice of working memory and executive function, degrading performance on unrelated tasks.
research.behavior_change: Log a specific next step ('follow up Thursday at 3pm') — not a vague reminder. The specificity is what closes the cognitive loop.

coach_message_template: '{task_name}' has been waiting {days} days without a check-in. Florida State research found open-loop tasks quietly occupy working memory even when you're not looking at them — and the relief comes from making a plan, not completing the task. A logged next step is enough.

research.primary_citation: Baumeister (1998) ego depletion — micro-decisions about backlog items (should I do this? is this still relevant?) deplete executive function faster than actually doing the tasks.
research.supporting_citation: Sweller (1988) cognitive load theory — a large visible backlog imposes constant cognitive tax even when not actively being processed. Once the pile feels unmanageable, avoidance becomes the dominant response, producing nonlinear growth.
research.behavior_change: Three concrete decisions (delete, defer with date, or do this week) restore a sense of agency and interrupt the avoidance loop. Not clearing the backlog — just 3 decisions.

coach_message_template: Your overdue backlog has grown by {delta} tasks this week. Cognitive load research shows that once a pile feels unmanageable, avoidance becomes the dominant response — so backlogs accelerate, not shrink, on their own. You don't need to clear it; you need 3 decisions right now.

research.primary_citation: Mark, Gonzalez & Harris (2005) UC Irvine — average re-engagement time after any context switch is 23 minutes 15 seconds. This applies to voluntary switches, not just interruptions.
research.supporting_citation: Leroy (2009) attention residue theory — the key variable is not switch frequency but whether you finish before switching. Completing a task to a natural stopping point nearly eliminates attention residue. An unfinished task causes cognitive intrusion into the next task for 10–20 minutes.
research.behavior_change: Consolidate short tasks into batches (admin hour, email block). The goal is not fewer switches — it's finishing each item before switching.

coach_message_template: Today looks fragmented — lots of short task windows. UC Irvine research found it takes over 23 minutes to fully re-engage after switching. Sophie Leroy's research adds a counterintuitive finding: the key isn't fewer switches, it's finishing before you switch — that's what eliminates the attention hangover that bleeds into the next task.

research.primary_citation: Wieth & Zacks (2011) — COUNTERINTUITIVE: insight and creative problems are solved better at non-peak alertness. The mechanism: inhibitory control that sharpens analytical focus simultaneously suppresses creative association. At low alertness, inhibition weakens and remote associations become accessible.
research.supporting_citation: Oppezzo & Schwartz (2014) — walking increased divergent (creative) thinking output by 81% vs sitting. The key distinction: generative work (writing, ideation, planning) benefits from non-peak. Analytical/execution work (coding to spec, editing) benefits from peak.
research.behavior_change: Classify deep work as generative (ideas, writing, design) or analytical (execution, debugging, review). Schedule generative at non-peak, analytical at peak.

coach_message_template: Some of your deep work is scheduled during your usual trough window. A counterintuitive 2011 finding: if this is generative work (writing, planning, ideation), your non-peak time might actually be better — the mental inhibition that sharpens analytical focus suppresses creative association. What kind of thinking does this task actually need?

research.primary_citation: Lavie (1986) ultradian rhythm research — biological 90-minute performance cycles. Each cycle ends with a low-alertness phase during which cortisol and adenosine accumulate. Ignoring this phase carries the physiological cost into the next cycle, compounding degradation.
research.supporting_citation: Kaplan & Kaplan (1989) attention restoration theory — directed attention is only restored by genuine disengagement from goal-directed activity. Phone-based breaks use the same attentional system being rested; they do not restore capacity. Hunter & Wu (2016): morning breaks provide highest restorative value per minute.
research.behavior_change: Schedule explicit recovery blocks (walk, eyes-closed rest, non-screen activity). Phone scrolling is not a break for the attentional system — it uses the same resource.

coach_message_template: You have {hours} hours of focus work scheduled without a break. Peretz Lavie's ultradian research found that recovery phases happen every ~90 minutes whether you plan them or not — ignoring them just carries the cost into the next cycle. Also worth knowing: phone-based breaks use the same attention system you're trying to rest.

research.primary_citation: Tetlock (2015) Superforecasting — good calibration is a learnable skill built from one behavior: systematically comparing predictions to outcomes and updating priors. It is not innate talent.
research.supporting_citation: Good time estimation compounds: accurate estimators schedule more realistically, which increases completion rates, which improves estimation further. The feedback loop is self-reinforcing.
research.behavior_change: Continue logging estimated vs actual duration after tasks. The tracking behavior is the mechanism — not a side effect.

coach_message_template: Your time estimates have been {accuracy_pct}% accurate this week. Research on superforecasters shows this is built from one behavior: comparing predictions to outcomes and letting that update your priors. This is a genuine, rare skill — and it compounds over time.

research.primary_citation: Amabile & Kramer (2011) Progress Principle — analysis of 12,000 daily work diary entries from 238 professionals across 7 companies. Consistent progress on meaningful work was the single strongest predictor of intrinsic motivation, positive mood, and creative output — stronger than praise, recognition, clear goals, or autonomy.
research.supporting_citation: Fishbach & Dhar (2005) — identity framing ('I'm someone who follows through') sustains behavior after streaks break. Debt framing ('I need to keep the streak alive') collapses sharply after a single miss. Coach messages should use identity framing.
research.behavior_change: No action needed. The behavior sustaining this streak is already working.

coach_message_template: You've completed what you set out to do for {streak} days running. Harvard research analyzing 12,000 daily work diaries found consistent progress on meaningful work is the most powerful intrinsic motivator known — more than praise or clear goals. What you're building isn't a streak number; it's evidence about who you are.
